chunk_summary: This section outlines a structured clinical approach to assess stunted children, focusing on anthropometry, nutritional phenotypes, proportionality, syndromic cues, and functional screening to identify underlying causes and guide management strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the process of validating anthropometric measurements in stunted children.
Learn to classify nutritional phenotypes and identify urgent cases requiring stabilization.
Recognize proportionality and syndromic cues to differentiate between skeletal dysplasia, genetic syndromes, and chronic diseases.
Perform endocrine, developmental, and functional screenings to guide clinical decisions.
key_facts: Stunting is defined as height-for-age Z-score (HAZ) < -2.
MUAC (mid-upper arm circumference) should be measured in children aged 6–59 months.
Concurrent wasting and stunting (WaSt) or nutritional edema requires immediate stabilization.
Proportional short stature may indicate chronic disease or micronutrient deficiencies, while disproportion suggests skeletal dysplasia or genetic syndromes.
Endocrine screening includes thyroid enlargement, pubertal staging, and blood pressure assessment for renal disease.
reference_quote: Short stature in childhood: challenges and choices — 'A structured clinical examination is essential to differentiate between proportionate and disproportionate short stature and identify underlying causes.'
document_key_topics: Endocrine and functional screening in stunted children
